title: AI Homework Help: Use It Right

modules.title: Shortcut or Cheat? You Decide
modules.sub: Explore where the line between help and dishonesty falls
modules.description: Learners step into a high-pressure homework scenario and make choices about how much to rely on AI. By playing through branching consequences, they begin to see how those choices affect their learning and their integrity.

modules.title: AI as a Tutor, Not a Ghost Writer
modules.sub: Discover how AI can explain without doing the work for you
modules.description: Learners practise using AI to ask questions, break down confusing concepts, and get unstuck — without handing over the whole assignment. They compare side-by-side examples of AI used as a tutor versus AI used as a replacement, and reflect on which actually builds their skills.

modules.title: The Credit Question: Who Did This?
modules.sub: Unpack authorship, ownership, and academic honesty with AI
modules.description: Learners examine real-world scenarios about crediting AI assistance and decide what counts as their own work. They explore school policies and ethical expectations, then practise writing honest attribution statements they could actually submit.

modules.title: Your Personal AI Homework Policy
modules.sub: Build a clear, honest set of rules you can stand behind
modules.description: Learners synthesise everything from the course to draft their own written policy for how they will and won't use AI on schoolwork. The finished policy is something they could confidently share with a teacher or parent, turning personal reflection into a concrete, defensible commitment.

synopsis: You're facing a deadline, a confusing assignment, and an AI tool that can write the whole thing in seconds — this story puts you in that moment and makes the consequences real. By playing through different choices, you'll develop your own honest, smart approach to using AI on schoolwork.
rationale: Students urgently need a practical ethical framework for AI-assisted schoolwork before schools and teachers define it entirely for them — literacy here means agency, not just compliance.
learning_outcome: Students can distinguish between AI uses that support their learning and those that replace it, and articulate a personal policy they could defend to a teacher or parent.
source_signals: The AI-Education Death Spiral a.k.a. Let the Kids Cheat
26% of students ages 13-17 are using ChatGPT to help with homework, study finds
His students suddenly started getting A's. Did a Google AI tool go too far? - CalMatters
His students suddenly started getting A's. Did a Google AI tool go too far? - Los Angeles Times
Student's Genius Exam Hack with AI Stuns Everyone! 🤯📚
